tier_2_handbook.definition: Occult primary cancer, also known as cancer of unknown primary (CUP), is defined as histologically confirmed metastatic tumors for which the primary site cannot be identified after standard pretreatment evaluation [1,2]. These tumors are epithelial in origin (carcinomas) and are characterized by early dissemination, aggressive behavior, and an unpredictable pattern of metastasis [3]. Common sites of metastasis include the liver, lungs, bones, and lymph nodes [18,19,20]. CUP accounts for approximately 2% to 9% of all cancer diagnoses and is among the 10 most frequently diagnosed tumors in developed countries [2,8,9]. The diagnosis is made after ruling out other possibilities such as lymphoma, melanoma, sarcoma, and germ cell tumors. CUP is associated with a poor prognosis, with a median overall survival of 8 to 12 months for most patients, although select patients with favorable subsets may have a median survival of 12 to 36 months [2,4,5]. In 20% to 50% of patients, the primary tumor is not identified even after postmortem examination [8,15,16]. A latent primary cancer may emerge during the natural course of the disease, although this is uncommon.

subtypes.name: Well- or moderately differentiated adenocarcinoma
subtypes.description: The most frequent histologic subtype, characterized by glandular differentiation and often presenting with metastases to liver, lungs, or bones.
subtypes.key_associations: Common primary sites include gastrointestinal tract (e.g., colon, stomach, pancreas), breast, lung, ovary, and prostate [2,22,26,27].

subtypes.name: Poorly differentiated adenocarcinoma
subtypes.description: A subtype with less glandular differentiation, often aggressive and associated with poor prognosis.
subtypes.key_associations: Primary sites are often difficult to identify; may originate from various organs, including pancreas, stomach, or lung [2,22].

subtypes.name: Squamous cell carcinoma (SCC)
subtypes.description: A subtype characterized by squamous differentiation, commonly involving lymph nodes in the head and neck or other regions.
subtypes.key_associations: Common primary sites include head and neck (e.g., oropharynx), lung, cervix, and skin [2,25,27].

subtypes.name: Undifferentiated carcinoma
subtypes.description: A highly aggressive subtype with no clear differentiation, often presenting with midline nodal distribution.
subtypes.key_associations: Primary sites are often unknown; may be associated with nasopharynx or other midline structures [2,22].

subtypes.name: Neuroendocrine tumors
subtypes.description: Tumors with neuroendocrine differentiation, which can be well-differentiated (carcinoid) or poorly differentiated (neuroendocrine carcinoma).
subtypes.key_associations: Common primary sites include pancreas, lung (e.g., small cell carcinoma), and gastrointestinal tract [2,222,223].

tier_2_handbook.molecular_pathogenesis: CUP exhibits complex molecular pathogenesis with multiple genetic alterations. Chromosomal abnormalities include activation of angiogenesis genes (50%–89% of tumors), oncogene overexpression (10%–30%), epithelial-to-mesenchymal transition marker elevation (16%), and activation of hypoxia-related proteins (25%) and intracellular signaling molecules (20%–35%) [2]. Overexpression of several genes is commonly observed, including EGFR, MET, JAK3, BRAF, PIK3CA, c-kit/PDGFR (PDGFRα or PDGFRβ), RAS, BCL2, HER2, and TP53 [9,28,29,30]. In a study of 252 patients, the most frequent genetic alterations were deletions in tumor suppressor genes TP53 (49.6%), CDKN2A (19.0%), and NOTCH1 (14.1%), and activation of oncogenes KRAS (23.4%), FGFR4 (14.9%), and PIK3CA (10.7%) [33]. KRAS activation and CDKN2A deletion were associated with poor prognosis. The BRD4-NUT oncogene, resulting from translocation t(15;19), is found in pediatric and young adult midline carcinomas [31,32]. Chromosomal instability may contribute to aggressive behavior [2,34]. These alterations provide potential targets for therapy, such as BRAF V600E, NTRK fusions, RET fusions, and HER2 amplification, guiding biomarker-driven treatments.

epidemiology.incidence: CUP accounts for 2% to 9% of all cancer diagnoses [2,8]. In the United States, an estimated 67,800 cases of cancer from other and unspecified primary sites are expected in 2026, representing approximately 3.2% of all cancers [10,11].
epidemiology.mortality: Deaths from CUP are estimated to reach 54,310 in 2026 in the United States [10].
epidemiology.trends: The incidence of CUP has been decreasing in recent years, likely due to improved diagnostics and detection of primary tumors, but survival rates have not improved [12,13].
epidemiology.demographics: Average age at diagnosis is 60 to 75 years. No specific sex ratio or racial disparities are explicitly reported in the source. Familial clustering is observed, with 2.8% of cases being familial [14].

risk_factors.detail: Smoking more than 20 cigarettes per day is strongly associated with increased CUP risk (multivariable adjusted HR, 1.45; 95% CI, 1.09–1.94; P trend = .02) compared to never smoking. Smoking duration ≥40 years also increases risk [17].
2.8% of CUP cases are familial, with associations with lung, kidney, and colorectal cancers in families, suggesting shared primary sites [14].
risk_factors.strength: Moderate, based on familial aggregation studies.

management_principles.overview: Occult primary tumors (cancers of unknown primary [CUP]) are histologically confirmed metastatic tumors whose primary site cannot be identified during standard pretreatment evaluation. These are heterogeneous tumors with a wide variety of clinical presentations and generally poor prognosis. The management philosophy is individualized, multidisciplinary, and goal-directed, focusing on symptom control and quality of life, especially in disseminated disease. The majority of CUP (approximately 80%) have a poor prognosis with a median overall survival (OS) of 3 to 10 months. However, certain favorable prognostic subsets exist, including patients with a single small resectable tumor, poorly differentiated carcinoma with midline nodal distribution, squamous cell carcinoma (SCC) of cervical lymph nodes, isolated inguinal adenopathy (SCC), poorly differentiated neuroendocrine carcinomas, women with papillary adenocarcinoma of the peritoneal cavity, women with adenocarcinoma involving only axillary lymph nodes, and men with blastic bone metastases and elevated prostate-specific antigen (PSA). For these patients, tailored treatment approaches, such as locoregional therapies or specific systemic therapy regimens, are recommended and may prolong survival.

by_intent.patient_group: Patients with localized disease amenable to locoregional therapy, such as a single resectable metastasis or limited nodal disease.
by_intent.approach: Treatment with curative intent may involve surgical resection, radiation therapy (RT), or both, often in conjunction with systemic therapy, and is based on the most likely primary site as guided by workup findings.

by_intent.patient_group: The majority of patients with disseminated metastases and a poor prognosis.
by_intent.approach: Treatment goals are symptom control, quality of life, and potentially prolonging survival. Systemic therapy is generally palliative. Preferred options include enrollment in a clinical trial, consideration of systemic therapy on an individual basis, and specialized approaches (e.g., targeted therapies, novel RT, palliative care).

management_principles.mdt_requirements: Multidisciplinary team (MDT) discussion is essential for the evaluation and management of CUP. The panel endorses enrollment of patients in appropriate clinical trials when possible. Communication between the clinician and pathologist is critical for directing immunohistochemistry (IHC) staining and molecular testing. Consultation with surgical, medical, and radiation oncologists, pathologists, radiologists, and other subspecialties (e.g., gynecologic oncology for suspected ovarian primary) is recommended as appropriate based on clinical presentation and workup findings.
management_principles.performance_status_guide: Performance status (PS) is a critical factor in determining treatment eligibility and intensity. For systemic therapy, the guidelines recommend therapy primarily for symptomatic patients with PS 1–2 or asymptomatic patients with PS 0 and aggressive cancer. Intensive regimens like FOLFIRINOX are reserved for patients with PS 0–1. Surgical resection for metastatic disease is considered for patients with good PS. Palliative RT is considered for symptomatic patients regardless of PS.

management_pathways.title: Adenocarcinoma/Carcinoma NOS: Predominant and Isolated Cervical Nodes

nodes.criteria: Adenocarcinoma or carcinoma not otherwise specified (Carcinoma NOS) with predominant and isolated cervical node metastases.

options.label: Follow workup for isolated cervical nodes
options.recommendation: Perform neck/chest/abdomen/pelvis CT if not done. Consider endoscopy if clinically indicated. Perform appropriate IHC stains. Treat per NCCN Guidelines for Head and Neck Cancers if primary is found.

options.label: Treat as per Head and Neck Cancers
options.recommendation: If a head and neck primary is identified or suspected, manage according to the NCCN Guidelines for Head and Neck Cancers.

adjuvant_logic.summary: Not applicable at this stage; treatment is directed by the identified or suspected primary site.

management_pathways.title: Adenocarcinoma/Carcinoma NOS: Supraclavicular Nodes
branching_basis: potential primary site (breast, lung, GI, etc.)

nodes.criteria: Adenocarcinoma/Carcinoma NOS with supraclavicular node involvement.

options.label: Additional Workup
options.recommendation: Perform neck/chest/abdomen/pelvis CT if not done. Consider endoscopy if clinically indicated. Perform appropriate IHC. Consider mammogram (intact breast tissue) and PSA (>40 y with prostate). Treat per NCCN disease-specific guidelines if primary found.

options.label: Locoregional/Systemic therapy
options.recommendation: Consider radiation therapy (RT) to the nodal basin if clinically indicated. Systemic therapy may be considered based on clinical scenario and patient factors.

adjuvant_logic.summary: Adjuvant considerations are integrated into the primary site-specific guidelines.

nodes.criteria: Adenocarcinoma/Carcinoma NOS with axillary node involvement.

options.label: Breast-focused workup
options.recommendation: Perform mammogram (in those with intact breast tissue including gynecomastia). If nondiagnostic and histopathologic evidence for breast cancer, breast MRI and/or breast ultrasound indicated. Perform appropriate IHC. Consider PSA (>40 y with prostate).

options.label: Treat per Breast Cancer Guidelines
options.recommendation: If breast cancer is confirmed or highly suspected, treat per NCCN Guidelines for Breast Cancer Screening and Diagnosis and NCCN Guidelines for Breast Cancer.

adjuvant_logic.summary: Treatment of the axillary node disease is dictated by the management of the underlying breast primary.

management_pathways.title: Adenocarcinoma/Carcinoma NOS: Mediastinum
branching_basis: potential primary site (germ cell, lung, breast, ovary)

nodes.criteria: Adenocarcinoma/Carcinoma NOS with mediastinal involvement.

options.label: Age-stratified workup
options.recommendation: Perform chest/abdomen/pelvis CT if not done. Test beta-hCG and alpha-fetoprotein. Consider testicular ultrasound if markers elevated (in those with testes, <50 y). Perform appropriate IHC. Consider mammogram and PSA.

options.label: Age-based treatment decision
options.recommendation: <40 y: Treat as poor-risk germ cell tumor per NCCN Guidelines for Testicular Cancer or Ovarian Cancer. 40 to <50 y: Treat as poor-risk germ cell tumor OR per NCCN Guidelines for Non-Small Cell Lung Cancer. ≥50 y: Treat per NCCN Guidelines for Non-Small Cell Lung Cancer.

adjuvant_logic.summary: Adjuvant therapy is integrated into the primary site-specific treatment protocols (e.g., germ cell or lung).

nodes.criteria: Adenocarcinoma/Carcinoma NOS with pleural effusion.

options.label: Additional Workup
options.recommendation: Perform chest/abdomen/pelvis CT if not done. Perform appropriate IHC. Consider gynecologic oncologist consult if CA-125 is elevated or clinically indicated (in those with a uterus and/or ovaries present). Consider mammogram and PSA.

options.label: Based on pathology
options.recommendation: Pathology defined: Treat as per appropriate NCCN Guidelines Treatment by Cancer Type. Other: Clinical trial preferred. Consider systemic therapy. Symptom control. Consider treating as lung primary (stage IVA) if not positive for another tumor marker per NCCN Guidelines for Non-Small Cell Lung Cancer.

adjuvant_logic.summary: Management is directed by the established or suspected primary site.

nodes.criteria: Adenocarcinoma/Carcinoma NOS with peritoneal metastases or malignant ascites.

options.label: Additional Workup
options.recommendation: Perform chest/abdomen/pelvis CT if not done. Perform urine cytology; cystoscopy if suspicious. Perform appropriate IHC. Check CA-125 (in those with uterus/ovaries present). Surgical or gynecologic oncologist consult as appropriate. Consider mammogram and PSA.

options.label: Based on pathology
options.recommendation: Pathology defined: Treat as per appropriate NCCN Guidelines Treatment by Cancer Type. Other: Clinical trial preferred. Consider systemic therapy. Symptom control.

adjuvant_logic.summary: Adjuvant therapy is part of the primary site-specific regimen if applicable.

management_pathways.title: Adenocarcinoma/Carcinoma NOS: Retroperitoneal Mass
branching_basis: histology subtype (germ cell vs non-germ cell)

nodes.criteria: Adenocarcinoma/Carcinoma NOS with retroperitoneal mass.

options.label: Additional Workup
options.recommendation: Perform chest/abdomen/pelvis CT if not done. Urine cytology; consider cystoscopy if suspicious. Perform appropriate IHC. Consider gynecologic oncologist consult if CA-125 elevated (in those with uterus/ovaries present). <65 y: Test beta-hCG, alpha-fetoprotein, testicular ultrasound (in those with testes). Consider mammogram and PSA.

options.label: Based on histology
options.recommendation: Histology consistent with germ cell tumor: Treat as poor-risk germ cell tumor per NCCN Guidelines for Testicular Cancer or Ovarian Cancer. Non-germ cell histology: Surgery and/or RT. Consider systemic therapy for selected patients.

adjuvant_logic.summary: For germ cell tumors, adjuvant therapy may be part of the treatment protocol.

nodes.criteria: Adenocarcinoma/Carcinoma NOS with unilateral inguinal node involvement.

options.label: Additional Workup
options.recommendation: Perform chest/abdomen/pelvis CT if not done. Perform appropriate IHC. Consider proctoscopy if clinically indicated. Check CA-125 (in those with uterus/ovaries present). Consider gynecologic oncologist consult. Consider PSA.

options.label: Unilateral management
options.recommendation: Lymph node dissection. Consider RT if clinically indicated ± systemic therapy.

adjuvant_logic.summary: Systemic therapy may be used in an adjuvant setting based on clinical judgment.

nodes.criteria: Adenocarcinoma/Carcinoma NOS with bilateral inguinal node involvement.

options.label: Bilateral management
options.recommendation: Bilateral lymph node dissection. Consider RT if clinically indicated ± systemic therapy (category 2B for RT alone).

adjuvant_logic.summary: Adjuvant systemic therapy may be considered after bilateral dissection.

nodes.criteria: Adenocarcinoma/Carcinoma NOS with liver metastases.

options.label: Additional Workup
options.recommendation: Perform chest/abdomen/pelvis CT if not done. Perform endoscopic evaluation. Check alpha-fetoprotein. Perform appropriate IHC. Consider mammogram and further liver-directed imaging per HCC/Biliary guidelines.

options.label: Resectable
options.recommendation: Surgical resection ± systemic therapy. If surgery is medically contraindicated, treat as unresectable.

options.label: Unresectable
options.recommendation: Treat as disseminated disease and/or consider locoregional therapeutic options per NCCN Guidelines for Hepatocellular Carcinoma.

adjuvant_logic.summary: Systemic therapy may be used in an adjuvant setting after resection.

nodes.criteria: Adenocarcinoma/Carcinoma NOS with bone metastases. Isolated lesion, painful lesion, or lesion with potential for fracture in weight-bearing area.

options.label: Additional Workup
options.recommendation: Perform chest/abdomen/pelvis CT with bone scan. Perform appropriate IHC. Consider mammogram and PSA.

options.label: Local therapy
options.recommendation: Surgery for impending fracture (in patients with good PS) and/or RT.

adjuvant_logic.summary: Local therapy is for the specific lesion; systemic control is a separate consideration.

nodes.criteria: Adenocarcinoma/Carcinoma NOS with multiple bone metastases or other disseminated disease.

options.label: Systemic and supportive
options.recommendation: Clinical trial preferred. Consider systemic therapy. Symptom control.

adjuvant_logic.summary: Management is for systemic disease.

nodes.criteria: Adenocarcinoma/Carcinoma NOS with brain metastases.

options.label: CNS metastases management
options.recommendation: See NCCN Guidelines for Central Nervous System Cancers for management of CNS metastatic lesions.

adjuvant_logic.summary: Adjuvant therapy is determined by the treatment of the brain metastases and systemic disease.

nodes.criteria: Adenocarcinoma/Carcinoma NOS with disseminated metastases not amenable to local therapy.

options.label: Palliative intent
options.recommendation: Symptom control. Clinical trial preferred. Consider systemic therapy on an individual basis. Specialized approaches (e.g., targeted therapy based on biomarkers, palliative care).

adjuvant_logic.summary: Therapy is palliative and not adjuvant.

management_pathways.title: Squamous Cell Carcinoma: Head and Neck Nodes

nodes.criteria: SCC with metastases in head and neck nodes.

options.label: Head and neck workup
options.recommendation: Follow NCCN Guidelines for Head and Neck Cancers for workup. Check p16 IHC, HPV ISH, and EBV ISH.

options.label: Treat per Head and Neck Guidelines
options.recommendation: Treat per NCCN Guidelines for Head and Neck Cancers.

adjuvant_logic.summary: Adjuvant therapy is part of the head and neck cancer treatment paradigm.

nodes.criteria: SCC with supraclavicular node involvement.

options.label: Additional Workup
options.recommendation: Perform chest/abdomen/pelvis CT. Perform endoscopy as indicated. Check p16/HPV and EBV status.

options.label: Locoregional/Systemic
options.recommendation: Consider RT to the nodal basin if clinically indicated ± systemic therapy.

adjuvant_logic.summary: Systemic therapy may be used in an adjuvant setting.

nodes.criteria: SCC with axillary node involvement.

options.label: Additional Workup
options.recommendation: Perform chest CT. Perform appropriate IHC. Consider complete skin exam.

options.label: Locoregional/Systemic
options.recommendation: Axillary node dissection. Consider RT if clinically indicated ± systemic therapy.

adjuvant_logic.summary: Systemic therapy may be used in an adjuvant setting.

nodes.criteria: SCC with mediastinal involvement.

options.label: Additional Workup
options.recommendation: Perform chest/abdomen/pelvis CT. Perform appropriate IHC.

options.label: Treat per Lung Cancer Guidelines
options.recommendation: Treat per NCCN Guidelines for Non-Small Cell Lung Cancer.

adjuvant_logic.summary: Adjuvant therapy is integrated into the lung cancer treatment protocol.

nodes.criteria: SCC with unilateral inguinal node involvement.

options.label: Additional Workup
options.recommendation: Perform abdomen/pelvis CT. Perform careful perineal and lower extremity exam. Gynecologic oncologist consult. Anoscopy. Consider cystoscopy. Check p16/HPV and EBV status.

options.label: Unilateral management
options.recommendation: Lymph node dissection. Consider RT if clinically indicated ± systemic therapy.

adjuvant_logic.summary: Systemic therapy may be used in an adjuvant setting.

nodes.criteria: SCC with bilateral inguinal node involvement.

options.label: Bilateral management
options.recommendation: Bilateral lymph node dissection. Consider RT if clinically indicated ± systemic therapy (category 2B for RT alone).

adjuvant_logic.summary: Adjuvant systemic therapy may be considered after bilateral dissection.

nodes.criteria: SCC with bone metastases. Isolated, painful, or lesion with potential for fracture in weight-bearing area.

options.label: Additional Workup
options.recommendation: Perform bone scan if only chest/abdomen/pelvis CT previously done. Diagnostic imaging studies for painful lesions and/or bone scan-positive lesions.

options.label: Local therapy
options.recommendation: Surgery for impending fracture (in patients with good PS) and/or RT.

adjuvant_logic.summary: Local therapy is for the specific lesion; systemic control is a separate consideration.

nodes.criteria: SCC with multiple bone metastases or other disseminated disease.

options.label: Systemic and supportive
options.recommendation: Clinical trial preferred. Consider systemic therapy. Symptom control.

adjuvant_logic.summary: Management is for systemic disease.

items.text: Complete history and physical (H&P), including breast, genitourinary, pelvic, rectal, skin, and/or oral cavity exam as appropriate, with attention to and review of past biopsies or malignancies, removed lesions, spontaneously regressing lesions, and existing imaging studies.
Complete blood count (CBC).
Comprehensive metabolic panel (CMP).
Lactate dehydrogenase (LDH) as indicated.
Chest/abdomen/pelvis CT scan with contrast (MRI with and without IV contrast or FDG-PET/CT if contraindication to contrast enhancement).
Clinically directed endoscopy, as indicated.
Core needle biopsy (preferred) and/or fine-needle aspiration (FNA) with cell block of most accessible site.
Consult pathologist for adequacy of specimen and additional studies including immunohistochemical (IHC) stains.
Biomarker testing using next-generation sequencing (NGS) (or other technique to identify gene alterations or tumor mutational burden [TMB]).
Microsatellite instability (MSI) or mismatch repair (MMR) testing.
HER2 IHC.
Tissue of origin studies are not recommended.

For cervical/supraclavicular/axillary nodes: Neck/chest/abdomen/pelvis CT (if not done); endoscopy if clinically indicated; appropriate IHC; mammogram (in those with intact breast tissue); PSA (>40 y in those with a prostate).
For mediastinum: Chest/abdomen/pelvis CT; beta-hCG, alpha-fetoprotein; appropriate IHC; mammogram and PSA; testicular ultrasound if markers elevated (<50 y with testes).
For pleural effusion/peritoneal ascites: Chest/abdomen/pelvis CT; appropriate IHC; CA-125 (in those with uterus/ovaries present); gynecologic oncologist consult; mammogram and PSA.

For retroperitoneal mass: Chest/abdomen/pelvis CT; urine cytology, cystoscopy if suspicious; appropriate IHC; gynecologic oncologist consult; CA-125; beta-hCG, alpha-fetoprotein, testicular ultrasound (<65 y with testes); mammogram and PSA.
For inguinal nodes: Chest/abdomen/pelvis CT; appropriate IHC; proctoscopy if indicated; CA-125; gynecologic oncologist consult; PSA; anoscopy (for SCC).
For liver: Chest/abdomen/pelvis CT; endoscopic evaluation; alpha-fetoprotein; appropriate IHC; mammogram and PSA; consider further liver-directed imaging.
For bone: Chest/abdomen/pelvis CT with bone scan; appropriate IHC; mammogram; PSA.
For brain: See NCCN Guidelines for Central Nervous System Cancers; chest/abdomen/pelvis CT; appropriate IHC; mammogram.
For multiple sites: Chest/abdomen/pelvis CT; appropriate IHC; mammogram; PSA.

surgery.role: Surgical resection is a component of curative-intent treatment for localized disease and for oligometastatic disease with resectable lesions in patients with good performance status. It is also used for tissue diagnosis.
principles: Surgical resection is considered for isolated, resectable metastases (e.g., liver, lung, bone with impending fracture) in patients with good PS.
Lymph node dissection is recommended for localized nodal disease (e.g., axillary, inguinal).
Surgery for impending fracture is recommended for bone metastases in weight-bearing areas in patients with good PS.
Surgical management of brain metastases follows NCCN Guidelines for Central Nervous System Cancers.

procedures.name: Lymph node dissection
procedures.approach: Surgical excision of involved lymph node basin (axillary, inguinal).
procedures.indications: Localized adenocarcinoma or SCC involving axillary or inguinal lymph nodes.

procedures.name: Surgical resection of metastasis
procedures.approach: Resection of isolated metastatic lesion (e.g., liver, lung).
procedures.indications: Solitary or limited metastases in patients with good PS and no evidence of disseminated disease.
procedures.key_complications: Organ-specific surgical risks (e.g., bleeding, infection, organ dysfunction).

procedures.name: Surgery for impending fracture
procedures.approach: Orthopedic fixation or stabilization of bone at risk of pathologic fracture.
procedures.indications: Bone metastasis with potential for fracture in a weight-bearing area in patients with good PS.
procedures.key_complications: Surgical site infection, hardware failure, persistent pain.

surgery.resectability_criteria: Resectability is determined by the ability to achieve a complete (R0) resection with acceptable morbidity and in patients with adequate performance status. For liver metastases, factors include number, size, and location of lesions, as well as sufficient future liver reserve. For lung nodules, complete resectability is key. For bone, surgery is for stabilization of impending fracture rather than oncologic resection.
surgery.pre_operative_optimisation: Optimization of performance status, nutritional status, and management of comorbidities is critical. For liver surgery, assessment of hepatic functional reserve is essential. For bone surgery, optimization of pain control and stabilization.

radiation_therapy.role: RT is used for definitive treatment of localized disease, adjuvant treatment after surgery, and palliation of symptoms.
principles: Consider definitive RT for patients with active localized disease.
Consider adjuvant RT after lymph node dissection if disease is limited to a single nodal site with extranodal extension or inadequate nodal dissection with multiple positive nodes.
Consider palliative RT for symptomatic patients (e.g., uncontrolled pain, impending pathologic fracture, cord compression).
Stereotactic body RT (SBRT) or stereotactic ablative radiotherapy (SABR) can be considered for limited (1–3) metastases and pulmonary metastases.

dose_frameworks.name: Adjuvant RT for nodal basin
dose_frameworks.indication: After lymph node dissection for isolated supraclavicular, axillary, or inguinal nodal metastasis with extranodal extension or inadequate dissection. May include a boost of 5–9 Gy.

dose_frameworks.name: Palliative RT hypofractionation
dose_frameworks.total_dose: 8 Gy in 1 fraction, or 20 Gy in 4–5 fractions, or 30 Gy in 10 fractions
dose_frameworks.indication: Symptomatic metastases (e.g., pain, impending fracture, cord compression).

approaches.name: Intensity-Modulated Radiation Therapy (IMRT)
approaches.dose_fractionation: As per standard dose frameworks; allows for conformal dose delivery.
approaches.concurrent_chemotherapy: May be used with concurrent systemic therapy depending on primary site and clinical scenario.
approaches.indication: For improved dose conformity and reduced toxicity, particularly for head and neck, pelvic, and other complex anatomical sites.
approaches.key_trial: Retrospective studies (e.g., Janssen et al., Kamal et al., Mourad et al.) show promising locoregional control and survival with IMRT for CUP with cervical node metastases.
approaches.toxicities: Mucositis, xerostomia (for head/neck), dysphagia, neck stiffness, trismus.

systemic_therapy.overview: Systemic therapy for CUP is generally palliative. The choice of regimen is based on histologic type (adenocarcinoma vs. SCC), clinical presentation, performance status, and biomarker status. Regimens are categorized as Preferred, Other Recommended, or Useful in Certain Circumstances. Biomarker-driven therapies are recommended for specific molecular alterations.

by_stage_and_setting.setting: Adenocarcinoma/Carcinoma NOS - First-line for disseminated disease
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: Platinum/taxane or platinum/gemcitabine combinations are standard empiric regimens with moderate activity (ORR ~30-40%, median OS ~10-12 months in favorable subsets).
by_stage_and_setting.alternatives: Capecitabine, Carboplatin/Docetaxel, Carboplatin/Gemcitabine, or for presumed GI primary: CAPEOX, FOLFIRI, mFOLFOX6.

by_stage_and_setting.setting: Adenocarcinoma/Carcinoma NOS - Presumed GI primary site
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: These are standard GI cancer regimens with activity in GI-type adenocarcinomas.
by_stage_and_setting.alternatives: Capecitabine, Roswell Park Fluorouracil/Leucovorin, Simplified Fluorouracil/Leucovorin, Weekly Fluorouracil/Leucovorin.
by_stage_and_setting.biomarker_requirements: None for standard use, but biomarker testing (e.g., RAS, BRAF) may inform targeted therapy.

by_stage_and_setting.rationale: Pembrolizumab and dostarlimab-gxly have tumor-agnostic approvals for dMMR/MSI-H solid tumors, showing durable responses (ORR ~34-42%).
by_stage_and_setting.biomarker_requirements: Must have dMMR or MSI-H status confirmed by IHC or PCR.

by_stage_and_setting.rationale: Pembrolizumab has an FDA approval for TMB-H solid tumors (ORR ~29%).
by_stage_and_setting.biomarker_requirements: TMB-High as determined by a validated and/or FDA-approved assay.

by_stage_and_setting.rationale: These NTRK inhibitors have tumor-agnostic approvals with high response rates (ORR ~57-75%).
by_stage_and_setting.biomarker_requirements: NTRK 1/2/3 gene fusion-positive without a known acquired resistance mutation.

by_stage_and_setting.rationale: This combination has an FDA approval for BRAF V600E-mutated solid tumors (ORR ~38%).
by_stage_and_setting.biomarker_requirements: BRAF V600E mutation-positive unresectable or metastatic solid tumors that have progressed following prior treatment.

by_stage_and_setting.rationale: Selpercatinib has an FDA approval for RET fusion-positive solid tumors (ORR ~44%).
This antibody-drug conjugate has an FDA approval for HER2-positive solid tumors (ORR ~61%).

by_stage_and_setting.setting: Squamous Cell Carcinoma - First-line for disseminated disease
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: Platinum/taxane is a standard regimen; FOLFOX is used for esophageal/gastric SCC.
by_stage_and_setting.alternatives: Capecitabine, Carboplatin/Docetaxel, Carboplatin/Gemcitabine, Cisplatin/Docetaxel, Cisplatin/Fluorouracil, Cisplatin/Gemcitabine, Cisplatin/Paclitaxel, or fluoropyrimidine-based regimens.

by_stage_and_setting.setting: Squamous Cell Carcinoma - Biomarker-selected (dMMR/MSI-H or TMB-H)
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: Pembrolizumab is indicated for dMMR/MSI-H or TMB-H tumors.

by_stage_and_setting.setting: Squamous Cell Carcinoma - Biomarker-selected (NTRK 1/2/3, BRAF V600E, HER2+, RET fusion)
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: Tumor-agnostic targeted therapies apply to SCC with these alterations.

key_regimens.name: Carboplatin/Paclitaxel
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Neutropenia, thrombocytopenia, anemia, alopecia, neuropathy, nausea, fatigue.

key_regimens.name: Cisplatin/Gemcitabine
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Myelosuppression, nausea, vomiting, renal toxicity, ototoxicity.

key_regimens.name: CAPEOX
key_regimens.setting: Adenocarcinoma/Carcinoma NOS with presumed GI primary, Preferred
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Hand-foot syndrome, diarrhea, nausea, neuropathy, myelosuppression.

key_regimens.name: FOLFIRI
key_regimens.setting: Adenocarcinoma/Carcinoma NOS with presumed GI primary, Preferred

drugs.name: Fluorouracil
drugs.dose: 400 mg/m2 IV bolus, then 1200 mg/m2/day IV continuous infusion x 2 days

key_regimens.name: mFOLFOX6
key_regimens.setting: Adenocarcinoma/Carcinoma NOS with presumed GI primary or SCC, Preferred

drugs.name: Fluorouracil
drugs.dose: 400 mg/m2 IV bolus, then 1200 mg/m2/day IV continuous infusion x 2 days

key_regimens.name: Pembrolizumab for dMMR/MSI-H
key_regimens.setting: Adenocarcinoma/Carcinoma NOS or SCC with dMMR/MSI-H
key_regimens.cycle_days: Repeat every 3 weeks, OR 400 mg IV or 4 mg/kg IV every 6 weeks.
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Immune-mediated adverse reactions (pneumonitis, colitis, hepatitis, endocrinopathies), fatigue, rash, pruritus.

key_regimens.name: Pembrolizumab for TMB-H
key_regimens.setting: Adenocarcinoma/Carcinoma NOS or SCC with TMB-H
key_regimens.cycle_days: Repeat every 3 weeks, OR 400 mg IV or 4 mg/kg IV every 6 weeks.

key_regimens.name: Dostarlimab-gxly
key_regimens.cycle_days: Repeat every 3 weeks for 4 doses, then 1000 mg every 6 weeks.
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Immune-mediated adverse reactions, fatigue, diarrhea, anemia.

key_regimens.name: Entrectinib
key_regimens.setting: Adenocarcinoma/Carcinoma NOS or SCC with NTRK gene fusion
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Fatigue, dizziness, dysgeusia, edema, cognitive effects.

key_regimens.name: Larotrectinib
key_regimens.setting: Adenocarcinoma/Carcinoma NOS or SCC with NTRK gene fusion

key_regimens.name: Repotrectinib
key_regimens.setting: Adenocarcinoma/Carcinoma NOS or SCC with NTRK gene fusion

drugs.name: Repotrectinib
drugs.day: Once daily for 14 days, then twice daily

key_regimens.key_toxicities: Peripheral neuropathy, cognitive effects, dyspnea, fatigue.

key_regimens.name: Selpercatinib
key_regimens.setting: Adenocarcinoma/Carcinoma NOS or SCC with RET gene fusion
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Hypertension, elevated transaminases, edema, diarrhea, dry mouth.

key_regimens.name: Dabrafenib/Trametinib
key_regimens.setting: Adenocarcinoma/Carcinoma NOS with BRAF V600E mutation
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Fever, rash, fatigue, nausea, peripheral edema, hypertension.

key_regimens.name: Fam-trastuzumab deruxtecan-nxki
key_regimens.setting: Adenocarcinoma/Carcinoma NOS or SCC with HER2-positive (IHC 3+) tumors
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Nausea, fatigue, alopecia, interstitial lung disease (ILD), myelosuppression.

key_regimens.name: Cisplatin/Fluorouracil
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Mucositis, myelosuppression, nausea, vomiting, renal toxicity.

key_regimens.name: Cisplatin/Fluorouracil with Concurrent Radiation
key_regimens.setting: Squamous Cell Carcinoma, Useful in Certain Circumstances (chemoradiation)

treatment_response_assessment.title: Follow-up for All Occult Primaries
treatment_response_assessment.timing: Follow-up frequency is determined by clinical need. For patients with no evidence of clinical disease, imaging every 6–12 months for up to 5 years.
response_logic: For patients with active disease or in remission, follow-up should be determined by clinical need.
History and physical examination are the cornerstones of follow-up.
Diagnostic tests should be directed by symptoms.
imaging_recommendations: For patients with no evidence of clinical disease, clinical follow-up with imaging every 6–12 months for up to 5 years is warranted.
Imaging modalities and frequency should be tailored to the treated sites and suspected primary.
biopsy_or_salvage_logic: Biopsy of new or growing lesions may be indicated to assess for recurrence, secondary primary, or transformation.
Salvage therapy should be considered for relapsed or refractory disease, ideally within the context of a clinical trial.

For patients with active and incurable disease, psychosocial support, symptom management, end-of-life discussions, palliative care interventions, and hospice care should be considered and utilized as appropriate.

prognosis.overall: CUP has a poor prognosis with a median overall survival (OS) of 8 to 12 months, though it varies significantly based on prognostic factors. Select patients with favorable subsets have a median OS of 12 to 36 months.2,4,5

by_stage.context: Median OS 3 to 10 months. Adenocarcinomas and undifferentiated tumors have worse prognosis than SCC (3.5% vs. 41.6% 3-year survival).2

Includes patients with a single, small, potentially resectable tumor; poorly differentiated carcinoma with midline nodal distribution; SCC of cervical lymph nodes (2%–5% of cases); isolated inguinal adenopathy (SCC); poorly differentiated neuroendocrine carcinomas (PDNECs); women with papillary adenocarcinoma of the peritoneal cavity; women with adenocarcinoma involving only axillary lymph nodes; and men with blastic bone metastases and elevated PSA.2,22,26,27

prognostic_factors: Gender assigned at birth (male gender is unfavorable)
ECOG performance status (PS) (poor PS is unfavorable)
Number of metastatic sites (multiple organs is unfavorable)
Presence of specific metastatic patterns (e.g., multiple cerebral metastases, nonpapillary malignant ascites)
prognosis.prognostic_context: A robust prognostic model and nomogram to predict OS in CUP was developed using five independent factors: gender, ECOG PS, histology, number of metastatic sites, and neutrophil-lymphocyte ratio. ECOG PS and neutrophil-lymphocyte ratio were the strongest predictors (P < .001). The model had a concordance probability estimate (CPE) of 0.69 and concordance index of 0.71.5

disease_related.management: Palliative radiotherapy (hypofractionated RT: e.g., 8 Gy x 1, 20 Gy x 4-5, or 30 Gy x 10 fractions) and systemic therapy as indicated.24

disease_related.complication: Impending pathologic fracture (e.g., from bone metastases)
disease_related.management: Surgery (in patients with good performance status) and/or radiotherapy.24
Empathetic discussion, support, and counseling by the oncology team and specialized services. Refer to NCCN Guidelines for Distress Management.124
Treatment per NCCN Guidelines for Central Nervous System Cancers.10

chemotherapy: Hematologic toxicities (neutropenia, anemia, thrombocytopenia) common with platinum-based and combination regimens (e.g., carboplatin/paclitaxel, gemcitabine/cisplatin).140,151
Significant grade 3/4 leukopenia (21%), neutropenia (33%), anemia (25%), thrombocytopenia (20%) with irinotecan/carboplatin.174
Overall higher toxicity with addition of etoposide to paclitaxel/carboplatin.141
immunotherapy: Treatment-related adverse events (any grade) in 64.8% of patients (14.6% grade 3–5) with pembrolizumab.188

Common adverse events: fatigue, anemia, diarrhea, nausea. Immune-mediated adverse events (e.g., pneumonitis, colitis, hepatitis, endocrinopathies) can occur with pembrolizumab and dostarlimab-gxly.188,192,193
targeted_therapy: Serious treatment-related adverse events in 40% of patients with selpercatinib; most common grade 3+: hypertension (22%), increased ALT (16%), increased AST (13%).195
Grade 3–4 adverse events (e.g., febrile neutropenia) can occur with fam-trastuzumab deruxtecan-nxki.201
surgery: Surgical resection risks are not specified but are inherent to the procedure.
radiation: Chronic radiation-associated dysphagia (7% of patients) in head and neck CUP.235
Radiation-associated toxicities: grade 1 xerostomia, dysphagia, neck stiffness, trismus.236

supportive_care.overview: Patients with active and incurable CUP require comprehensive supportive care, including symptom management, palliative care interventions, and end-of-life discussions. Early initiation of these discussions is recommended.12
supportive_care.nutritional_support: Not explicitly detailed in the source.
supportive_care.anti_emetic_protocol: Not explicitly detailed in the source.
supportive_care.gcsf_guidance: Not explicitly detailed in the source.
supportive_care.vte_prophylaxis: Not explicitly detailed in the source.
supportive_care.pain_management: Consider palliative radiotherapy for symptomatic patients with uncontrolled pain, impending pathologic fracture, or cord compression.24 Refer to NCCN Guidelines for Adult Cancer Pain.12
supportive_care.psychosocial_support: Empathetic discussion about the natural history, prognosis, and provision of support and counseling by the primary oncology team and specialized services to alleviate distress. Use NCCN Distress Thermometer and Problem List. Refer to NCCN Guidelines for Distress Management.124
supportive_care.dental_care: Not explicitly detailed in the source.

follow_up.post_curative_treatment: For patients with localized disease in remission, follow-up frequency is determined by clinical need, consisting of history and physical examination, with diagnostic tests for symptomatic patients. For patients with no evidence of clinical disease, clinical follow-up with imaging every 6–12 months for up to 5 years is warranted.12
follow_up.surveillance_rationale: To monitor for disease recurrence, detect late effects of treatment, and manage survivorship issues. The primary goal is symptom control and quality of life in incurable disease.12
late_effects_screening: Screening for treatment-related toxicities (e.g., neuropathy, renal toxicity from platinum, cardiac toxicity from anthracyclines) based on prior therapies.
Monitoring for secondary malignancies related to radiation or chemotherapy.
follow_up.recurrence_patterns: Patterns vary by tumor type and primary site. A latent primary cancer may emerge during the natural course, though uncommon. In 20% to 50% of patients, the primary tumor is not identified even after postmortem examination.8,15,16

clinical_follow_up_schedule: For patients with no evidence of clinical disease: clinical follow-up with imaging every 6–12 months for up to 5 years.12
For patients with active disease or in remission: follow-up frequency determined by clinical need.12
imaging_strategy: Initial evaluation: contrast-enhanced chest/abdomen/pelvis CT. FDG-PET/CT is an alternative only if contrast is contraindicated.85
Follow-up: imaging as indicated by symptoms and clinical status.
Consider FDG-PET/CT in select situations, especially when considering local or regional therapy.85
laboratory_monitoring: Initial: complete blood count (CBC), comprehensive metabolic panel (CMP), lactate dehydrogenase (LDH) as indicated.1
Disease-specific markers as indicated (e.g., PSA for prostate, CA-125 for ovarian, AFP for liver).1
supportive_follow_up: Psychosocial support and distress management per NCCN Guidelines for Distress Management.
Palliative care and symptom management per NCCN Guidelines for Palliative Care and Adult Cancer Pain.
Survivorship care per NCCN Guidelines for Survivorship.

special_situations.management: Treat per NCCN Guidelines for Breast Cancer Screening and Diagnosis and NCCN Guidelines for Breast Cancer.8

special_situations.situation: Adenocarcinoma with mediastinal nodes in patients <50 years
special_situations.key_message: Suggests possible testicular germ cell tumor.
special_situations.management: Treat as poor-risk germ cell tumor per NCCN Guidelines for Testicular Cancer or germ cell tumor per NCCN Guidelines for Ovarian Cancer.8

special_situations.key_message: Common site for occult primary SCC (2%–5% of cases).25
special_situations.management: Treat per NCCN Guidelines for Head and Neck Cancers.12

special_situations.key_message: Highly responsive to cisplatin-based chemotherapy, with potential for long-term survival.222
special_situations.management: Treat per NCCN Guidelines for Small Cell Lung Cancer (for poorly differentiated/high-grade or small cell subtype) or NCCN Guidelines for Neuroendocrine and Adrenal Tumors (for well-differentiated).8
Dabrafenib/Trametinib for patients who have progressed following prior treatment and have no satisfactory alternative treatment options.22
special_situations.evidence: Based on NCI-MATCH trial subprotocol H (Phase II).22

special_situations.management: Pembrolizumab or dostarlimab-gxly (for dMMR/MSI-H) for patients with recurrent/advanced disease following prior treatment.23,188
special_situations.evidence: Based on KEYNOTE-158 and GARNET trials.188,193

special_situations.management: Entrectinib, larotrectinib, or repotrectinib for metastatic tumors without alternative treatments or that have progressed.27,28,29
special_situations.evidence: Based on clinical trials (STARTRK-1, STARTRK-2, ALKA-372-001, TRIDENT-1).27,28,29

special_situations.key_message: Tumor-agnostic approval for HER2-directed antibody-drug conjugate.
special_situations.management: Fam-trastuzumab deruxtecan-nxki for patients who have progressed on or following prior systemic treatment and have no satisfactory alternative treatment options.31
special_situations.evidence: Based on DESTINY-PanTumor02 phase II trial.31

clinical_pearls: CUP is a heterogeneous group of metastatic tumors with an unknown primary site, accounting for 2%–9% of all tumors.2,8

The initial workup should include a core needle biopsy (preferred) or FNA with cell block, immunohistochemistry (IHC) staining, microsatellite instability (MSI) or mismatch repair (MMR) testing, HER2 IHC, and biomarker testing using next-generation sequencing (NGS).1
Treatment is tailored to the most likely primary site if identified through workup; otherwise, systemic therapy is chosen based on histologic type and biomarker status.1

For patients with disseminated disease, the preferred treatment option is enrollment in a clinical trial. Systemic therapy is generally palliative and should be limited to symptomatic patients (PS 1–2) or asymptomatic patients (PS 0) with aggressive cancer.1
Favorable prognostic subsets exist (e.g., SCC of cervical nodes, poorly differentiated carcinomas with midline distribution) and may benefit from specific, potentially curative therapies.22,26,27

Biomarker testing is crucial: dMMR/MSI-H or TMB-H tumors respond to pembrolizumab; BRAF V600E to dabrafenib/trametinib; NTRK fusions to TRK inhibitors; RET fusions to selpercatinib; HER2 3+ to fam-trastuzumab deruxtecan-nxki.22,23,27,29,31,32
Supportive and psychosocial care are integral components of management, with early referral to palliative care and distress management services recommended.124
Follow-up for patients with no evidence of clinical disease includes imaging every 6–12 months for up to 5 years.12

key_trials.full_name: Efficacy of Pembrolizumab in Patients With Noncolorectal High Microsatellite Instability/Mismatch Repair-Deficient Cancer
key_trials.population: Patients with 27 different MSI-H/dMMR tumor types (endometrial, gastric, cholangiocarcinoma, pancreatic most common) following progression on ≥1 prior therapy.
key_trials.intervention: Pembrolizumab 200 mg IV every 3 weeks or 400 mg IV every 6 weeks.
key_trials.key_result: ORR 34.3%, median PFS 4 months, median OS 24 months.
key_trials.practice_change: Supported tumor-agnostic approval of pembrolizumab for MSI-H/dMMR solid tumors.

key_trials.full_name: A Phase 1, Multi-Cohort Study of Dostarlimab in Patients With Advanced Solid Tumors
key_trials.population: Patients with dMMR recurrent or advanced solid tumors that progressed on or following prior treatment and who had no satisfactory alternative treatment options.
key_trials.intervention: Dostarlimab-gxly 500 mg IV every 3 weeks for 4 doses, then 1000 mg IV every 6 weeks.
key_trials.key_result: ORR 42% (9% complete response, 33% partial response), median duration of response 35 months.
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: Common treatment-related adverse events: fatigue, anemia, diarrhea, nausea. Immune-mediated adverse events occurred.
key_trials.practice_change: Supported FDA approval of dostarlimab-gxly for dMMR advanced solid tumors.

key_trials.full_name: Tumour-Agnostic Efficacy and Safety of Selpercatinib in Patients With RET Fusion-Positive Solid Tumours Other Than Lung or Thyroid Tumours
key_trials.population: Patients with locally advanced or metastatic RET fusion-positive solid tumors (other than NSCLC and thyroid) that progressed on or following prior systemic treatment or who had no satisfactory alternative treatment options.
key_trials.intervention: Selpercatinib 160 mg PO twice daily (or 120 mg twice daily if <50 kg).
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: Serious treatment-related adverse events in 40%; most common grade 3+: hypertension (22%), increased ALT (16%), increased AST (13%).
key_trials.practice_change: Supported FDA approval of selpercatinib for RET fusion-positive solid tumors.

key_trials.full_name: A Phase 1/2 Study of Repotrectinib in Patients With ROS1 or NTRK Fusion-Positive Advanced Solid Tumors
key_trials.population: Patients with NTRK fusion-positive locally advanced or metastatic solid tumors who had progressed following prior systemic treatment.
key_trials.intervention: Repotrectinib 160 mg PO once daily for 14 days, then 160 mg PO twice daily.
key_trials.practice_change: Supports repotrectinib as an option for NTRK fusion-positive tumors after prior TRK inhibitor.

key_trials.full_name: Efficacy and Safety of Trastuzumab Deruxtecan in Patients With HER2-Expressing Solid Tumors: Primary Results From a Phase II Trial
key_trials.n: 267 (with 5 CUP patients; primary analysis in HER2 IHC 3+ subgroup)
key_trials.population: Patients with HER2-expressing (IHC 3+/2+) locally advanced or metastatic solid tumors who had received ≥1 systemic treatment or had no alternative treatment options.
key_trials.intervention: Fam-trastuzumab deruxtecan-nxki 5.4 mg/kg IV every 21 days.
key_trials.primary_endpoint: ORR per RECIST v1.1 by blinded independent central review.
key_trials.key_result: In HER2 IHC 3+ subgroup: ORR 61.3% (95% CI 49.4–72.4), median OS 21.1 months.
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: Not detailed for CUP subgroup specifically.
key_trials.practice_change: Supported FDA approval for HER2-positive solid tumors.

key_trials.full_name: Dabrafenib and Trametinib in Patients With Tumors With BRAF V600E Mutations: Results of the NCI-MATCH Trial
key_trials.population: Patients with BRAF V600E-mutated unresectable or metastatic solid tumors (excluding melanoma, thyroid, colorectal, and NSCLC) that progressed following prior treatment and had no satisfactory alternative treatment options.
key_trials.intervention: Dabrafenib 150 mg PO twice daily plus trametinib 2 mg PO daily.
key_trials.key_result: ORR 38% (90% CI 22.9–54.9%; P < .0001 vs. null rate of 5%). Median PFS 11.4 months, average OS 28.6 months.
key_trials.practice_change: Supported FDA approval of dabrafenib/trametinib for BRAF V600E-mutated solid tumors.

key_trials.full_name: Molecularly Guided Therapy Versus Chemotherapy After Disease Control in Unfavourable Cancer of Unknown Primary
key_trials.population: Patients with non-squamous, histologically confirmed CUP who achieved response (complete, partial, or stable disease) to induction chemotherapy.
key_trials.intervention: Molecularly guided therapy (MGT) based on NGS results for patients positive for genomic alterations; chemotherapy plus atezolizumab for patients negative for genomic alterations.
key_trials.comparator: Continued empiric platinum-based chemotherapy for at least three additional cycles.
key_trials.key_result: PFS 6.1 months (95% CI 4.7–6.5) in MGT arm vs. 4.4 months (95% CI 4.1–5.6) in chemotherapy arm. In patients positive for genomic alterations, median PFS was 8.1 months (95% CI 4.6–8.7) for MGT vs. 4.7 months (95% CI 4.0–6.6) for chemotherapy. Median OS was 14.7 months vs. 11.0 months.
key_trials.practice_change: Demonstrated clinical benefit of NGS-directed therapy after induction in CUP.
